revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Iwai Onsen]][[ja:岩井温泉]] {{Expand Japanese|岩井温泉}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Iwai Onsen | native_name = 岩井温泉 | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Iwai Onsen | coordinates = {{coord|35.55552778|134.36224444}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Iwai Onsen'''|岩井温泉}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Inaba Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Onsen|lt=onsen|ar|أونسن|az|Onsen|bcl|Onsen|ca|Onsen|cs|Onsen|da|Onsen|de|Onsen|el|Όνσεν|en|Onsen|eo|Onsen|es|Onsen|et|Onsen|eu|Onsen|fa|اونسن|fi|Onsen|fr|Onsen|he|אונסן|hu|Onszen|id|Onsen|it|Onsen|ja|温泉|km|អុនសេន|ko|일본의 온천|la|Onsen|lt|Onsenas|lv|Onsens|ms|Onsen|my|အွန်းဆန်း|nl|Onsen|nn|Onsen|no|Onsen|pl|Onsen|pt|Onsen|ro|Onsen|ru|Онсэн|simple|Onsen|sk|Onsen|sl|Onsen|sv|Onsen|ta|ஒன்சென்|th|อนเซ็ง|tl|Onsen|tr|Onsen|uk|Онсен|ur|اونسن|vi|Onsen|zh|日式溫泉|qid=Q655311|1=Onsen}} shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Q134917287|ltq=Q134917287|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} * {{ill|Shikinai Hiteisha|lt=Shikinai Hiteisha|ja|比定社|qid=Q135026601|1=Shikinai Hiteisha}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|Q11420254|ltq=Q11420254|qid=Q11420254|1=List of Shikinaisha in Inaba Province}} ** Series ordinal: 7 ** P155: {{ill|Q21654507|ltq=Q21654507|qid=Q21654507|1=Futagami-yama Shrine (Tottori Prefecture)}} ** P156: {{ill|Hinono Shrine|lt=Hinono Shrine|qid=Q135040724|1=Hinono Shrine}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q127864|ltq=Q127864|qid=Q127864|1=Chūgoku region}} * {{ill|Q133935|ltq=Q133935|qid=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}} * Iwami|ja|岩美郡|ko|이와미군|pl|Powiat Iwami|sr|Област Ивами|zh|岩美郡|qid=Q1131619|1=Iwami District, Tottori}} * {{ill|Q281925|ltq=Q281925|qid=Q281925|1=Iwami, Tottori}} * {{ill|Inaba Province|lt=Inaba Province|cs|Provincie Inaba|de|Provinz Inaba|en|Inaba Province|es|Provincia de Inaba|fa|ولایت اینابا|fr|Province d'Inaba|gl|Provincia de Inaba|id|Provinsi Inaba|it|Inaba (provincia)|ja|因幡国|ko|이나바국|lt|Inabos žemė|nl|Inaba (provincie)|pt|Província de Inaba|ru|Инаба (провинция)|simple|Inaba Province|sk|Inaba (provincia)|sr|Провинција Инаба|th|แคว้นอินาบะ|tr|Inaba (vilayet)|uk|Провінція Інаба|zh_min_nan|Inaba-kok|zh|因幡國|qid=Q1054338|1=Inaba Province}} * {{ill|Q11049025|ltq=Q11049025|qid=Q11049025|1=Iwai district}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|35.55552778|134.36224444}} == country (P17) == * ᥓᥣᥱ ᥙᥣᥢᥱ|tet|Japaun|te|జపాన్|tg|Ҷопон|th|ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|thnews|หมวดหมู่:ประเทศญี่ปุ่น|tig|ጃፓን|ti|ጃፓን|tk|Ýaponiýa|tl|Hapon|tly|Japonijə|tn|Japan|tok|ma Nijon|to|Siapani|tpi|Siapan|trv|Tanah Tunux|tr|Japonya|trnews|Kategori:Japonya|trquote|Japonya|trvoyage|Japonya|ts|Japani|tt|Япония|tum|Japan|tw|Japan|tyv|Япония|ty|Tāpōnē|udm|Япония|ug|ياپونىيە|uk|Японія|uknews|Категорія:Японія|ukquote|Японія|ukvoyage|Японія|ur|جاپان|uz|Yaponiya|vec|Japon|vep|Japonii|ve|Japan|vi|Nhật Bản|vibooks|Các quốc gia/Nhật Bản|vivoyage|Nhật Bản|vls|Japan|vo|Yapän|war|Hapon|wa|Djapon|wo|Sapoŋ|wuu|日本|xal|Ниxуудин Нутг|xh|IJapani|xmf|იაპონია|yi|יאפאן|yo|Japan|za|Nyiedbonj|zea|Japan|zgh|ⵏⵉⴱⵓⵏ|zh_classical|日本|zh_min_nan|Ji̍t-pún|zh_yue|日本|zh|日本|zhnews|Category:日本|zhquote|日本|zhvoyage|日本|zu|IJaphani|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == name in kana (P1814) == * いわいおんせん * ミユノEngishiki Jinmyōchō == temperature (P2076) == * {'amount': '+50', 'unit': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q25267'} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '鳥取県岩美郡岩美町岩井字宮谷141', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:岩井温泉]] {{wikidata link|Q11474068}} == Japanese content == {{Japanese hot springs |Name = Iwai Onsen |Image = Iwaionsen-tottori1200.jpg| |Comment = |Location = {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|qid=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwami District|Q1131619|qid=Q1131619|1=Iwami District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Iwami Town|Q281925|qid=Q281925|1=Iwami, Tottori}} {{Location map many | Japan Tottori Prefecture | relief = 1 | caption = | width = 220 | float = center | lat_deg = 35.555155 | lon_deg = 134.362856 | pos = top| label = Iwai Onsen | lat2_deg = 35.494006 | lon2_deg = 134.225888 | mark2 = blue pog.svg | pos2 = bottom | label2 = {{ill|lt=Tottori Station|Q858295|qid=Q858295|1=Tottori Station}} }}
Tottori prefecture map
|Latitude degrees =35 |Latitude minutes =33 |Latitude seconds = 18.6 |longitude degrees =134 |longitude minutes =21|longitude seconds = 46.3 |Transportation access = Train/Bus: {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|qid=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Iwami Station|Q899398|qid=Q899398|1=Iwami Station (Tottori)}} Approximately 10 minutes by {{ill|lt=Nippon Kotsu (Tottori)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nikko Bus}}
Car: Approximately 30 minutes east from central Tottori city on {{ill|lt=National Route 9|Q1055522|qid=Q1055522|1=Japan National Route 9}}|Spring quality = {{ill|lt=sulfate spring|Q11587674|qid=Q11587674|1=Sulfate spring}} (salt-containing gypsum spring) |Spring temperature = 50℃ |Spring volume = |pH = |Humoral classification = |Osmotic pressure classification = |Number of accommodation facilities = 3 |Total capacity = |Annual number of bathers = 12,249 people |Statistical year = 2017 (Heisei 29) |External link = [http://www.inabaspa.net/spa/iwai/ Iwai Onsen | Tottori Inaba Onsenkyo] |Special notes = }} {{Location map | Japan Tottori | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 33 | lat_sec = 19.9 | lat_dir = N | lon_deg = 134 | lon_min = 21 | lon_sec = 44.08 | lon_dir = E | label = Iwai Onsen location | position = bottom | mark = Map pointer.svg | marksize = 9 | width = 300 | float = right | caption = Location of Iwai Onsen | alt = Iwai Onsen location }}'''Iwai Onsen'' is located in {{ill|lt=Tottori Prefecture|Q133935|qid=Q133935|1=Tottori Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Iwami District|Q1131619|qid=Q1131619|1=Iwami District, Tottori}}{{ill|lt=Iwami Town|Q281925|qid=Q281925|1=Iwami, Tottori}} (former country {{ill|lt=Inaba Province|Q1054338|qid=Q1054338|1=Inaba Province}}). It is said to have a history of 1,300 years, and has a unique bathing method called ``Yukamuri.'' . On March 30, 1973 ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}48), it was designated as {{ill|lt=National Recreation Hot Spring Area|Q11421080|qid=Q11421080|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas}}. === Spring quality === *Old name - Gypsum spring containing gypsum (Gypsum bitter spring , Bitter mirabilite spring ) *New notation - {{ill|lt=sulfate spring|Q11587674|qid=Q11587674|1=Sulfate spring}} :(*Refer to {{ill|lt=Izumi quality|Q11554939|qid=Q11554939|1=Hot Spring Types}} for the new and old notation) === Hot spring town === It is located in an open area where several tributaries join the {{ill|lt=Gamo River (Tottori Prefecture)|Q21652810|qid=Q21652810|lt=Gamo River|1=Gamo River}}, and the hot spring town is formed on the left and right sides of a side street that branches off from {{ill|lt=National Route 9|Q1055522|qid=Q1055522|1=Japan National Route 9}}. There are 3 accommodation facilities. There is one {{ill|lt=public bath|Q11392653|qid=Q11392653|1=Communal hot springs}}, the town-run Yukamuri Onsen. === Short history === Iwai Onsen was formerly called ``Gamoyu'' (located along the {{ill|lt=Gamo River (Tottori Prefecture)|Q21652810|qid=Q21652810|lt=Gamo River|1=Gamo River}}), ``Ginyu'', and ``Shimane no Oyu''. ;The legend of the opening of the hot springs It is said to have opened in {{ill|lt=859|Q23897|qid=Q23897|1=859}} ({{ill|lt=Jokan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} first year), and is counted as one of the ``Eight Old Hot Springs'' of the Heian period.According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|qid=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, a descendant of {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Fuyutsugu|Q3090616|qid=Q3090616|1=Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu}}. According to "Zenkoku Onsen Guide" published in 1923, he is the second son of Fuyutada Fujiwara, a descendant of Fuyutsugu. In }}, there was a man named Fuyuhisa Fujiwara, who suffered from a skin disease at a young age. Fuyuhisa goes on a journey in despair, but he finds a hot spring with the sacred staff of a {{ill|lt=shrine maiden|Q1047139|qid=Q1047139|1=Miko}} that he met in Iwai, and is said to have been cured of his illness. Fuyuhisa later carved a statue of Yakushi Nyorai using a shrine maiden as a model, and according to another theory, it was created by {{ill|lt=Saichō|Q449943|qid=Q449943|1=Saichō}} of {{ill|lt=Mt. Hiei|Q748040|qid=Q748040|1=Mount Hiei}}}} and made it the principal image of '''Togenji''''. There are different theories about the circumstances that led Fuyuhisa to leave Kyoto and come to this area. Fuyuhisa was an intelligent person and was loved by his mother. The mother tried to get rid of her eldest son and set up her second son, Fuyuhisa, but Fuyuhisa thought that would be against morality, so he disguised himself as a madman, left home, and set out on a wandering journey. When he reached Iwai, he was guided by a beautiful woman who was the embodiment of the medical king and opened a hot spring. There is also a {{ill|lt=legendary of the opening of the hot springs|Q11655277|qid=Q11655277|1=Onsen Origin Myth}} that says that {{ill|lt=Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773|qid=Q854773|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} caused the hot spring to gush out. ; "Uji" Fuyuhisa named the area ``Uji'' after his hometown in Kyoto, {{ill|lt=Uji City|Q326956|qid=Q326956|lt=Uji|1=Uji}}, guided the cultivation of fields, and was revered by the common people as ``Uji Choja.'' The current ``Uji'' district is located on the opposite bank of the Gamo River from the hot spring town. Jogan 2nd year (860Accurately speaking, the ``2nd year of Jogan'' was from ``January 27, 860'' to ``January 15, 861'' according to the Julian calendar, and the ``2nd year of Jogan'' corresponds to 860 or 861.) The reputation of the hot springs became known to {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|qid=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, who granted timber and land to Fuyuhisa. As a result, a hot spring bathtub was constructed, and since then, Fuyuhisa's descendants have been managing the hot spring for generations. ;Devastation and revival of hot springsAccording to the Zenkoku Onsen Guide, hot springs became obsolete at the end of the Kamakura period due to conflicts associated with {{ill|lt=Shochu no Incident|Q11007252|qid=Q11007252|1=Shochu no Incident}} and {{ill|lt=Genko War|Q1432624|qid=Q1432624|1=Genkō War}}. It is also said that the source was buried due to the ``war turmoil during the Sengoku period.'' It was revived by {{ill|lt=Tottori Domain|Q905521|qid=Q905521|lt=Lord of Tottori|1=Tottori Domain}}{{ill|lt=Mitsunaka Ikeda|Q11551720|qid=Q11551720|1=Ikeda Mitsunaka}} in the Edo period. Although the area is close to the Sea of Japan, it is a little inland, so the climate is relatively mild.It is located at the foot of a mountain pass on the highway connecting the San'in area and Kyoto, and flourished as a hot spring town and a post town on the highway, attracting hot spring bathers. In the mid-Edo period, {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|qid=Q1195131|lt=Kansei period|1=Kansei}}, there were 16 inns and 8 hot springs, including one exclusively for the feudal lord. ;Modern times and beyondAt the end of the Meiji period, when the {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|qid=Q1135170|lt=railway|1=San'in Main Line}} was established, the number of tourists from the Keihanshin area increased. Because the railway was built along the coast rather than along the old highway route, the nearest station was located a little far away, so the locals built the {{ill|lt=#Iwai Light Railway|UNKNOWN|lt=Light Railway}} during the Taisho period to transport passengers. However, the ``Zenkoku Onsen Guide'' published in Taisho 13 states that it is only 30 towns (approximately 3 km) from the station and the path is flat, so you can walk while admiring the many famous and historic sites along the way. ===Togenji Temple=== [[File:Iwai-tougenji-tottori1200.jpg|right|250px|thumb|Togenji]] Togenji Temple is located in the center of Iwai Onsen, at the foot of Mt. Atago. According to Fujiwara Fuyuhisa's {{ill|lt=The legend of the opening of the hot springs|Q11655277|qid=Q11655277|1=Onsen Origin Myth}}, Togenji was founded by Fuyuhisa. It is said that in {{ill|lt=894|Q30577|qid=Q30577|1=894}}, {{ill|lt=Ennin|Q732282|qid=Q732282|1=Ennin}}, who returned from {{ill|lt=envoys to Tang China|Q262529|qid=Q262529|1=Japanese missions to Tang China}}, stopped by this temple and named it ``Yueizan Nyorai-ji Temple.'' was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1686|Q7664|qid=Q7664|1=1686}} ({{ill|lt=Jokyo|Q1146221|qid=Q1146221|1=Jōkyō}}3 year) as a branch temple of {{ill|lt=Maniji|Q11498610|qid=Q11498610|1=Mani-ji Temple}} in Tottori. The name was changed to ``Iouzan Yugenji,'' and later, the name was changed to ``Togenji,'' because it is located northeast of Inaba and is the source of hot springs. ===Oyu Shrine=== Onyu Shrine (Onyujinja, Miyujinja, Oyujinja) is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} that is said to have been founded in {{ill|lt=811|Q29946|qid=Q29946|1=811}} ({{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|qid=Q1373070|1=Kōnin (era)}}2). It currently stands behind the ruins of Iwai Abandoned Temple, with the mountain behind it, but it was once located in the Ohno district to the east . For this reason, even after the relocation, it was sometimes called "Oonomiya" . It was called "Isemiya" during the Edo period, but was renamed to Oyu Shrine in the Meiji period. The Kirin Lion Dance performed at the annual spring festival in March has been preserved as a traditional performing art. ===Yamamuri=== ``Yukamuri'' is a unique bathing method handed down in Iwai Onsen . During the hot spring treatment, one puts a tenugui over one's head, taps the hot water with a special ladle, and pours hot water over one's head while reciting the ``Yukamuri Uta'' . {{ill|lt=Toson Shimazaki|Q1093783|qid=Q1093783|1=Tōson Shimazaki}} mentioned Yukamuri Uta in his book ``San'in Souvenirs'' . {{SquoteH}} [[File:Shimazaki Toson2.jpg|left|100px]] We arrived at Iwai around sunset. Although it wasn't in the mountains as I had expected, the Akashiya hot spring inn where we stayed was located along a quiet rural road. Just thinking that I was in the middle of Inaba Province somehow made me feel better about traveling. The water was hot, but it was not uncomfortable. That night there was a summer-like moon, shining right onto the tatami mats on the second floor at the back of the inn. Even the dark persimmon leaves in the garden are cool with the moon shining here and there. I also keep thinking about my husband's home in Tokyo. Shoji also seemed to be traveling, so he brought along postcards from the hotel. ``Maybe I should send a postcard to Chiyan Kusunoki.''
"I'll ask you to go to Tokyo as well."
Shoji also wrote the travel advice on my behalf. I also asked the host of the inn to let me listen to something called a local hot spring song. Measles has been around for a long time, and local people have been singing songs while pouring hot water on their heads with ladles. The rhythm of the song originates from the sound of the ladle pouring hot water. It had a rustic feel to it, as if a dog were listening to it. This bath song was certainly one of the treats. Even though we were living in a mountainous area, we had hung mosquito nets at these inns, so Shoji and I slept late that night with our pillows lined up inside the mosquito net. '''Toson Shimazaki "San'in Souvenir" 4. Summer on the San'in Road''' {{SquoteF}} ===Railway=== From 1922 (Taisho 11) to 1943 (Showa 18), the public {{ill|lt=light railway|Q32680449|qid=Q32680449|1=Light railway}} line was in operation, connecting Iwai Onsen and {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|qid=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Iwami Station|Q899398|qid=Q899398|1=Iwami Station (Tottori)}}. The railroad ran to the mines south of Hot Springs. The area around Iwai Onsen was a key transportation hub during the Edo period, with an inn station that used the hot springs, but when the San'in Main Line was completed at the end of the Meiji period, the line no longer passed through Iwai Onsen, but instead ran north from Iwami towards the sea. The San'in Main Line brought many hot spring visitors from the Sanyo and Keihan areas, but because they passed through Iwai Onsen, it was decided to build a railway in the local village of Iwai to connect Iwai Onsen and Iwami Station. In {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|qid=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taisho 11), a village-run light railway opened, communicating with the Japanese National Railways and transporting hot spring visitors and ore along the Gamo River. In the past, it was crowded with hot spring visitors, but profits deteriorated due to competition with other hot spring resorts in Tottori Prefecture, the Great Iwai Fire of {{ill|lt=1934|Q18714|qid=Q18714|1=1934}} (1934), and the decline of the mining industry. As the current situation worsened, steel products were also brought under control, and it became impossible to repair the lines, so the line was closed in {{ill|lt=1943|Q18623|qid=Q18623|1=1943}} (Showa 18). It was never reopened after that and was abandoned. For details, see {{ill|lt=Iwai Town Tramway|Q11474073|qid=Q11474073|1=Iwai Town Tramway}}. ===Dynamics=== According to a survey conducted by Tottori Prefecture in {{ill|lt=2017|Q25290|qid=Q25290|1=2017}} (2017), the number of visitors taking baths per year was approximately 15,000 , which is less than half compared to around {{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|qid=Q1985|1=2000}}.. Although the total number of bathing visitors in Tottori Prefecture has decreased by approximately 260,000 people (approximately -20%) in the 10 years since {{ill|lt=2000|Q1985|qid=Q1985|1=2000}}, the decline has been even greater. The peak number of tourists was during the so-called {{ill|lt=bubble economy|Q6500827|qid=Q6500827|lt=bubble period|1=Japanese asset price bubble}} from the end of the Showa era to around 1993, when it is estimated that 120,000 tourists visited each year. (This number is estimated using a different method than the survey on the number of bathing customers mentioned above, and cannot be easily compared. group="Note">The number of bathing customers in Tottori Prefecture as a whole in {{ill|lt=2010|Q1995|qid=Q1995|1=2010}} was approximately 1.15 million, calculated from the bathing tax amount, and based on the same method, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=1998|1=UNKNOWN}} is approximately 1.55 million people, whereas the value for {{ill|lt=1997|Q2088|qid=Q2088|1=1997}} is approximately 6 million people using the old calculation method, which is a significant difference.. === Access === '''Public transportation''' * From {{ill|lt=San'in Main Line|Q1135170|qid=Q1135170|1=San'in Main Line}}{{ill|lt=Iwami Station|Q899398|qid=Q899398|1=Iwami Station (Tottori)}}, take the {{ill|lt=Nippon Kotsu (Tottori)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nikko Bus}} (Iwami/Iwai Line) bound for Hasebashi (route number: 33) or Kabushima (route number: 32/32H) for about 10 minutes. (Also available from {{ill|lt=Tottori Station|Q858295|qid=Q858295|1=Tottori Station}}). * Take the Uradome Coast/Tottori Sand Dunes Sightseeing Bonnet Bus from Tottori Station on the San'in Main Line and get off at the last stop (operates on Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays from spring to fall; only one round trip per day). '''Automobile''' *About 30 minutes east from central Tottori city on {{ill|lt=National Route 9|Q1055522|qid=Q1055522|1=Japan National Route 9}}. ===Surrounding facilities/sights=== *Uji Shrine - It was once called Hachimangu Shrine, but was renamed to Uji Shrine at the beginning of the Meiji period. is said to have been founded by Fujiwara Fuyuhisa. *Oyu Shrine - See {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=#Oyu Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. *Togenji Temple - See {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=#Togenji|1=UNKNOWN}}. *Midori Ozaki Museum - {{ill|lt=Midori Ozaki|Q11465252|qid=Q11465252|1=Midori Osaki}} is a novelist born in 1869 (Meiji 29) in Iwai Onsen. *Iwai Abandoned Temple Pagoda Ruins - A historical site from the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hakuho period|1=UNKNOWN}}. The Yakushi Nyorai statue that is said to have once existed here is located at {{ill|lt=Enzanji|Q11486594|qid=Q11486594|1=Ensan-ji Temple}} in {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|qid=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}, and has been designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|qid=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. ===Footnote=== {{Footnote help}} ===References=== *“National Hot Spring Guide”, Hot Spring Research Association, 1924 *“Hot Spring Guide”, Ministry of Railways, 1940 *Illustrated Japanese Cultural Geography Series 4 China 1, edited p560-563 *“Tottori Prefecture Place Names (Japanese Historical Place Name System)”, Heibonsha, 1992 *"Japanese Place Name Dictionary 31 Tottori Prefecture ({{ill|lt=Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|Q4093011|qid=Q4093011|1=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten}})", {{ill|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|Q1054765|qid=Q1054765|1=Kadokawa Shoten}}, 1982, ISBN 978-4040013107 *"Tottori Prefecture Encyclopedia", edited by Shin Nihonkai Shimbun Tottori Prefecture Encyclopedia Compilation Committee, Shin Nihonkai Shimbun, 1984, p74-75, "Iwai Hot Springs", "Iwai Hot Spring Discovery Legend", "Iwai Light Railway", "Iwai Great Fire" *[https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Culture and Tourism Bureau Tourism Policy Division 2010 Tourist Inflow Dynamics Survey The annual number of bathers is calculated from the bathing tax. *[https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Culture and Tourism Bureau, Tourism Policy Division, Tourist Inflow Dynamics Survey Results (2010)], pp. 13-14, accessed September 9, 2014. *[https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226511/3003onsenirikomi.pdf Tottori Prefecture Tourism Exchange Bureau Tourism Strategy Division Number of visitors to hot springs (March 2018)] Accessed June 7, 2018. *[http://www.inabapyonpyon.net/~housen/fada5.html Inaba Yakushi Sacred Hall Society Togenji] Accessed September 23, 2014. *[http://www.tottori-dentou.net/dantai_detail.php?id=132 Tottori Traditional Performing Arts Archives Iwai Oyu Shrine Kirin Lion Dance] Accessed September 23, 2014. *"Zenkoku Onsen Guide" p448-453 "Iwai Onsen" *"Hot Spring Guide" p208 "Iwai Onsen" *"Japanese Place Name Dictionary 31 Tottori Prefecture ({{ill|lt=Kadokawa Japanese Place Name Dictionary|Q4093011|qid=Q4093011|1=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten}})" p116 "Iwai Railway" }} === Related items === *{{ill|lt=Tourist spots in Tottori Prefecture|Q11674529|qid=Q11674529|1=Tourist spots in Tottori Prefecture}} === External link === {{Wiki portal link|Hot springs|[[Image:Hot springs 001.svg|34px|Portal:Hot springs]]}} * {{Wayback|url=http://www13.ocn.ne.jp/~yukamuri/ |title=Yukamuri Onsen |date=20040216171917}} - Town-run public bath * [http://www.saninji.jp/126 Sanin Tourism Federation Iwai Onsen] *[http://www.inabaspa.net Tottori Inaba Onsenkyo] {{Hot springs in Tottori Prefecture}} {{Hot spring}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Iwai Onsen]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Iwai Onsen]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Iwai Onsen]][[Category:Shikinaisha in Inaba Province]] [[Category:Hot springs of Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:岩美町]] [[Category:山陰海岸ジオパーク]]
